title: The Brian De Palma Top 10 and ‘Blow Out’ at 40
format: Sean Fennessey and critic Adam Nayman survey 50 years of Brian De Palma, then spend the back half on Blow Out, 40 years on.

segments.heading: The Case for Brian De Palma

films.title: Murder à la Mod
films.note: One of his earliest films, with direct-to-camera interview scenes he restaged 40 years later, on a much bigger budget, in The Black Dahlia.

films.title: Greetings
films.note: A draft-dodger comedy starring a young Robert De Niro, with a scene tracing the Zapruder film's bullet trajectory over a topless woman's body, years before Oliver Stone's JFK.

films.title: Hi, Mom!
films.note: Contains the “Be Black Baby” sequence, where a Black theater troupe stages an immersive show that puts white audience members in blackface — shot so it's unclear what's staged and what's real.

films.title: Dionysus in '69
films.note: A filmed record of an experimental theater troupe, from his mid-60s run of non-narrative, avant-garde work.

films.title: Get to Know Your Rabbit
films.note: His first studio picture and, by his own account, a disaster; the failure recurs as a theme across his later films.

films.title: Sisters
films.note: A Bernard Herrmann score and overt Hitchcock quotation tied him to the “De Palma is just doing Hitchcock” line of criticism that followed him into the '80s.

films.title: Phantom of the Paradise
films.note: A rock-musical/horror hybrid that flopped on release and has gained a cult following since.

films.title: Carrie
films.note: His commercial breakout, anchored by a Sissy Spacek performance the hosts say is played entirely straight while everything around her carries some irony.

films.title: Obsession
films.note: An underseen, unapologetically Hitchcockian thriller; Paul Schrader, who wrote it, also wrote Taxi Driver — reportedly with De Palma once attached to direct that too.

films.title: The Fury
films.note: Telekinetic twins and a government villain played by John Cassavetes; Pauline Kael's review argued the film's faults don't matter next to its best scenes.

films.title: Dressed to Kill
films.note: Widely hated on release for its violence and Hitchcock appropriation, but a hit relative to his budget — the film that fixed his reputation as a talented pain in the ass.

films.title: Scarface
films.note: A hit whose afterlife in hip-hop and pop culture dwarfs its actual reputation among De Palma's admirers; the climactic shootout was partly staged by a visiting Steven Spielberg.

films.title: Body Double
films.note: A power-drill murder set piece in a movie so loose it barely holds together — which is the point; American Psycho's Patrick Bateman calls it his favorite film.

films.title: The Untouchables
films.note: His most conventional studio picture, built around Kevin Costner's moral certainty, with a Battleship Potemkin homage staged almost shot-for-shot at a train station.

films.title: Casualties of War
films.note: A brutal, commercially unsuccessful Vietnam War film about a soldier's failure to stop a war crime; De Palma has said he has a hard time watching it himself.

films.title: The Bonfire of the Vanities
films.note: A notorious flop, chronicled in Julie Salomon's book The Devil's Candy.

films.title: Raising Cain
films.note: An explicit return to Dressed to Kill territory, with dreams nested inside flashbacks inside dreams.

films.title: Carlito's Way
films.note: Al Pacino in the film Cahiers du Cinéma called the best of the '90s; the hosts single out its extended tracking shots through the climax.

films.title: Mission: Impossible
films.note: Set the template the franchise still runs on — and its ending kills off most of the original TV show's cast, a meaner version of a plan De Palma wanted to push even further.

films.title: Snake Eyes
films.note: A Nicolas Cage vehicle that reads as a conventional Bruckheimer/David Koepp thriller on the surface but works the same conspiracy and surveillance ideas as Mission: Impossible.

films.title: Mission to Mars
films.note: The commercially middling sci-fi movie that inspired Armond White's line that not liking it means not understanding movies.

films.title: Femme Fatale
films.note: Opens with a sex scene in a Cannes bathroom stall while a worse movie plays in the theater — a litmus-test film for whether his sensibility is for you.

films.title: The Black Dahlia
films.note: Restages the direct-to-camera interview scenes from Murder à la Mod on a studio budget.

films.title: Redacted
films.note: Structured as found YouTube footage, it retells the same story as Casualties of War transplanted to Iraq; his fights with producer Mark Cuban over the ending are part of its history.

films.title: Passion
films.note: A stripped-down return to the Hitchcockian erotic-thriller mode of Dressed to Kill and Body Double.

films.title: Domino
films.note: A troubled, low-budget European co-production; the hosts hear a handful of scenes in it as good as anything else in his filmography.

films.title: De Palma
films.note: Noah Baumbach and Jake Paltrow's documentary, where De Palma is proudest discussing the films that made money — which the hosts find telling.

films.title: Blow Out
films.note: Released as Reagan took office, folding Chappaquiddick and the Zapruder film into an original plot: a sound man (John Travolta) obsessively reconstructs a car crash he recorded, opposite Nancy Allen, John Lithgow, and Dennis Franz. The hosts call it his most totally controlled film and single out the demagnetized-tapes scene and the finale's reuse of a scream recorded as sound-effect filler for Lawrence of Arabia.

referenced.group: What De Palma almost made instead of Blow Out
